Alright, let's go to section three and let's talk about visibility. The big point that I would like to make about this, it is that most people are not discriminatory enough about who they are seeking visibility with, who they are appealing to, who they are getting themselves in front of, the place or the marketplace that they are in. And most people prevent themselves getting to the top of the income ladder by being in front of the wrong people too much of the time. And so you have to sort of assess is where you are working, first of all giving you a sufficient number of people to do business with. Secondly, that they are willing and able to give you the sums of money you need to cobble together the income you want. And so over the years there have been places that were good for me that then weren't good for me for no reason other than I could get the sum there that I needed to cobble together the income that I wanted. So chiropractic. Now there are ways to get it, Chris franchising. But from my business model, so for a while, for example, bringing chiropractors into seminars, selling them marketing systems and out the back end picking off a consulting client here and there, a copywriting client here and there, plus we had a little factory that did their yellow pages ads and their brochures. That was a very nice business. But it wasn't seven favors because a single practice, a doc in a single practice, even if he does everything right, he's only going to bring so many patients through the doors in a year. He's only going to do so much money and he can afford to pay me X but there's no way he can afford to pay me Y. I got to be dealing with somebody else who can sensibly and reasonably afford to pay me Y, so I got to move on from that place and move to a different place. People are very reluctant to leave a place that they are comfortable in and confident in and have formed presence and reputation and relation in. And so they stay bound to it even though it can't meet this criteria and it cannot really support the income that they wish to have. And then they continue to invest resources in becoming more of a presence in this place that can't support them in the way that they want and need to be supported. So I'm no fan of presence for presence's sake. Visibility for visibility's sake. Fame for fame's sake except for a really big broad player. So it's useful to be famous just about anywhere if you're Oprah because you're pulling from just about anywhere and everywhere except maybe like, I don't know, male construction workers probably are not a good mix. But so most people, this is very logical, most people will acknowledge the logic of it. I've got to be where there's enough money to meet my goals, but then they still won't move because it's so uncomfortable to move. And so you need a realistic assessment of this, of who it is you're appealing to. And my argument, there's a great example which I won't take time to do, my argument is this, that audience is really everything. You can't afford, when you want to move up to the seven range, you really can't afford to be wasting time or money on any but the ideally suited. So again, to use an example, if I go back eight years, nine years, ten years,
